title: IBKR Portfolio Report v2 — Long/Short Metals Book

tradeTape.note: The book was rebuilt from the original 6-name long-only lithium basket into a 27-name long/short metals book — long lithium & specialty-materials producers vs. short precious/PGM, steel, aluminum and battery/EV ETFs. Rows show the largest current holdings at average entry cost (not intraday fills; live trade history needs an approved IBKR pull).

etfLookThrough.concentrationNote: Long lithium producers (+14.5% of NAV) while short the BATT/LIT/KARS ETFs (−11.9%) that themselves hold those same miners — a partial internal self-hedge. HHI 635 (~15.7 effective names): far more diversified than the retired 6-name book (HHI 1,924 / ~5.2).

metrics.note: from the account's daily TWR since inception; ~half the old book's 38% — the long/short structure damps it
~17 daily obs; a net-short metals book still carries market beta
low — long/short largely neutralises directional metals beta (old book 0.69)
90-day daily returns, 25 of 27 holdings (OTC lines ex.); well below the old book's 0.67 — real diversification from the long/short mix
single worst daily TWR in the ~4-week window
27 names, ~15.7 effective — well spread vs the old 1,924 / 5.2

scenarios.name: Steel complex −15%
scenarios.note: net short NUE/STLD/CLF/RS → gain · first-order est

drivers.read: Mixed — long ALB/SQM/LAC/SGML vs short BATT/LIT/KARS

driversNote: Net-short-friendly backdrop: precious metals (silver −21% 3M, gold −14%, PGM in surplus) and aluminum are correcting — a tailwind to the book's short precious/PGM/aluminum leg — while steel stays firm (HRC >$1,100 on 50% S232 tariffs), a headwind to the steel shorts, which are the book's largest bucket (~39% of gross). Lithium is two-sided. Macro: the Fed is on a dovish hold at 3.63%, CPI has eased to 3.46%, the curve is normal (10Y–2Y +0.40) and the VIX is calm at 16.5.

smartMoney.detail: Buy consensus, mean PT ~$216 (+71% upside), SmartScore 9 — supports the long lithium leg (though price momentum is weak).
Buy consensus, PT ~$270 (+19%), +27% vs 200DMA, composite 80.6 — the steel short is fighting a strong tape; the highest-rated steel name is the wrong one to be short.

options.note: The book holds no options — it is cash equities/ETFs, net short −33% of NAV. There is no embedded vega or convexity; the risk is linear and directional, captured by the scenarios above.

performance.note: Since inception (2026-06-22) the account's time-weighted return is −11.3%. Over the same window SPY returned +1.4% and the metals ETF XME −10.4% — the book tracked the metals complex down through the transition from the old long-only basket. The sample is ~4 weeks and spans the rebuild, so treat this as indicative, not a track record.

bottomLine: The book was rebuilt from a concentrated long-lithium bet into a diversified 27-name long/short metals book: net short −33% of NAV, gross ~70%, ~15.7 effective names (HHI 635 vs the old 1,924). Realised vol has roughly halved to ~19% and metals beta is low (XME 0.25) as the long/short structure neutralises directional metals exposure, though SPY beta stays ~0.9. Since inception (Jun 22) the account is −11.3% TWR, tracking XME (−10.4%) down through the transition. Crucially the book now PROFITS from a metals selloff (steel −15% ≈ +$570, broad base metals −10% ≈ +$500) and is hurt by a precious/steel squeeze — the opposite of the old long book. The largest risk bucket is steel (~39% of gross, all short) into Q2 earnings (CLF Jul 23, NUE/STLD late Jul), where the shorts fight a firm tape. Watch the Jul 28–29 FOMC and the lithium longs' prints (ALB early Aug, SQM Aug 19).
sources: IBKR (live account, refreshed 2026-07-16) · FMP · TipRanks · our micro.json commodity-bias & analyst layer · macro feeds. Risk metrics computed from the account's daily TWR series since inception (2026-06-22, ~18 daily obs) — a short sample spanning the book's rebuild, so vol/beta are indicative. Scenario P&L are first-order estimates (miner β≈1.3). Informational only — not investment advice.
